This means the LAW entity type as captured is not useful for RQ3c as intended. To decide what to do, I ran the following:

```{r}
entity_table %>%
  filter(entity_type == "LAW",
         !str_detect(entity, 
           "^section|^chapter|^subsection|^subdivision|^article|_code$|public_act")) %>%
  group_by(entity) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(n), bills = n_distinct(bill_id)) %>%
  slice_max(total, n = 20) %>%
  print(n = 20)
```

The output shows the problem is systemic, not fixable with more filtering:

page_2, page_5, page_4, page_11 — page number artifacts misclassified as laws

the_criminal_code_of — incomplete truncated phrase

title_7, title_eleven — title number references, not named laws

the_use_tax_act, occupation_tax_act, the_service_occupation_tax_act — tax law citations irrelevant to SDOH

act — too generic to mean anything

cpf_15570 — uninterpretable artifact

The only genuinely meaningful named law in the entire top 20 is social_security_act at 37 bills. That is far too thin a signal to justify keeping the LAW type, and it can be captured more reliably as a bigram in your n-gram table.

Therefore, I chose to Drop LAW entities entirely and rely on your n-gram table's domain column to capture references to structural legal frameworks. The medicaid ORG entity and dictionary terms like "managed care" and "health care coverage" already capture federal law references indirectly.

#### Final Entity Table Build

```{r}
# Drop LAW entirely — signal too weak after cleaning
entity_table <- entity_table %>%
  filter(entity_type != "LAW") %>%
  anti_join(org_stopwords, by = "entity")

# Confirm final entity type distribution
count(entity_table, entity_type, sort = TRUE)

# Final top 20 ORG — should now be clean
entity_table %>%
  filter(entity_type == "ORG") %>%
  group_by(entity) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(n), bills = n_distinct(bill_id)) %>%
  slice_max(total, n = 20) %>%
  print(n = 20)
```

Good progress — medicaid and the department entities are clean, but there are several more artifacts to remove. One more cleaning pass:

#### Add to org_stopwords and Re-filter

```{r}
org_stopwords <- bind_rows(org_stopwords, tibble(entity = c(
  "board",                                    # too generic
  "blank",                                    # formatting artifact
  "general_assembly_state",                   # fragmented preamble text
  "general_assembly_state_of",                # same
  "the_legislature_of_the_state_of_texas_:",  # preamble boilerplate with punctuation artifact
  "general_assembly",                         # procedural body
  "the_house_of_representatives",             # procedural body
  "lc",                                       # citation abbreviation artifact
  "l.s.d."                                    # abbreviation artifact
)))

entity_table <- entity_table %>%
  anti_join(org_stopwords, by = "entity")
```

Keep these — all are meaningful:

the_illinois_state_police — law enforcement institutional actor; relevant to social_community framing

medicare — structural federal health program alongside medicaid

the_interstate_commission — multi-state regulatory body; meaningful structural actor

mco — managed care organization; directly relevant to healthcare_access

the_state_department_of_health_care_services — California's Medicaid agency; meaningful

the_department_of_human_services, the_department_of_health, the_department_of_public_health — all confirmed structural actors

One ambiguous entry:
mco appears in 6 bills with 286 occurrences — that is a high frequency for only 6 bills, suggesting it dominates those specific bills. It is a legitimate managed care abbreviation, but check whether those 6 bills are all from one state:

```{r}
entity_table %>%
  filter(entity == "mco") %>%
  left_join(corpus_table %>% select(bill_id, state), by = "bill_id") %>%
  count(state)
```

All 6 are from one state (IL).

After re-filtering, run the top 20 ORG one final time and share it. The list should now be substantively clean throughout.

```{r}
entity_table %>%
  filter(entity_type == "ORG") %>%
  group_by(entity) %>%
  summarise(total = sum(n), bills = n_distinct(bill_id)) %>%
  slice_max(total, n = 20) %>%
  print(n = 20)
```

Three more to remove, then the ORG list is confirmed clean:

```{r}
org_stopwords <- bind_rows(org_stopwords, tibble(entity = c(
  "california_legislature",   # procedural body — state-specific preamble
  "assembly",                 # procedural body
  "no_fiscal_committee",      # CA bill header artifact
  "usc"                       # legal citation abbreviation (U.S. Code)
)))

entity_table <- entity_table %>%
  anti_join(org_stopwords, by = "entity")
```

Everything else is confirmed meaningful:

| Entity                                       | Why it stays                                                        |
| -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| medicaid                                     | Core healthcare access structural program                           |
| health_and_safety_code                       | CA regulatory framework reference                                   |
| the_department_of_human_services             | Social services structural actor                                    |
| the_illinois_department                      | IL agency reference — keep despite truncation                       |
| the_department_of_health                     | Healthcare structural actor                                         |
| the_department_of_public_health              | Same                                                                |
| the_illinois_state_police                    | Law enforcement institutional actor                                 |
| medicare                                     | Federal health program — structural                                 |
| the_interstate_commission                    | Multi-state regulatory body                                         |
| mco                                          | Managed care organization                                           |
| the_state_department_of_health_care_services | CA Medicaid agency                                                  |
| the_state_board_of_education                 | Education structural actor — meaningful for RQ3                     |
| medi_-_cal                                   | California's Medicaid program — equivalent to medicaid for CA bills |
| the_nursing_home_care_act                    | Long-term care structural framing                                   |
| the_welfare_and_institutions_code            | CA welfare structural framework                                     |
| the_health_and_safety_code                   | Duplicate of health_and_safety_code with article — keep both        |

### Named Entity Table: Confirmed Complete

```{r}
write_csv(entity_table, "data/entity_table.csv")
saveRDS(entity_table, "data/entity_table.rds")
spacy_finalize()
```

## Topic table

The topic table is built using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) from the topicmodels package, which you practiced in Lab 9 with the Associated Press dataset. The core workflow follows the same pattern: token counts → DTM → LDA model → extract gamma. The key difference for your project is that each bill is a document, and gamma becomes the proportion variable you need.

### Stage 1: Build Word Counts from Your Token Table

Your token table already exists and is clean — use it directly rather than re-tokenizing:

```{r}
library(topicmodels)
library(tidytext)
library(tidyverse)

# Reuse token table — filter to content-bearing words only
word_counts <- token_table %>%
  count(bill_id, token, sort = FALSE)
```

### Stage 2: Cast to a DTM

Just as in Lab 9, cast_dtm() converts the tidy one-token-per-row format into the Document-Term Matrix that LDA() requires:

```{r}
bills_dtm <- word_counts %>%
  cast_dtm(bill_id, token, n)

bills_dtm   # inspect dimensions — should show 3169 documents
```

Check for and remove empty documents before proceeding:

```{r}
# Remove rows with all zeros (bills that lost all tokens after cleaning)
bills_dtm <- bills_dtm[rowSums(as.matrix(bills_dtm)) > 0, ]
```

### Stage 3: Choosing K — A Critical Decision

Unlike the Lab 9 sanity check where k=4 was known in advance because there were four books, your corpus requires you to justify k empirically and theoretically. The Text as Data reading cautions that statistical fit metrics alone are insufficient — substantive fit must guide k selection.

Run candidate models at several values and compare:

```{r}
# Test k values — this will take time
candidate_k <- c(10, 15, 20, 25, 30)

perplexity_scores <- map_dbl(candidate_k, function(k) {
  model <- LDA(bills_dtm, k = k, 
               control = list(seed = 1234))
  perplexity(model, bills_dtm)
})

# Plot perplexity — look for the "elbow"
tibble(k = candidate_k, perplexity = perplexity_scores) %>%
  ggplot(aes(k, perplexity)) +
  geom_line() + geom_point() +
  labs(title = "LDA Perplexity by Number of Topics",
       x = "Number of Topics (k)", y = "Perplexity")
```

Recommended Value: k = 20
Here is the reasoning based on both the plot and your corpus characteristics:

The steepest perplexity drop occurs between k=10 and k=15 — those k values are too coarse for a corpus covering multiple states, years, and policy domains

The rate of decrease visibly slows between k=20 and k=25, suggesting diminishing substantive returns beyond k=20

With 3,169 bills spanning 8+ states across 4+ years, k=20 gives enough granularity to capture variation across states and time for RQ4 without producing topics too narrow to interpret

k=20 is consistent with topic modeling norms in political science legislative text analysis, where corpora of similar size typically use 15–25 topics

### Stage 4: Fit the Final LDA Model

```{r}
bills_lda <- LDA(bills_dtm, 
                 k = 20, 
                 control = list(seed = 1234))

bills_lda  # confirm: "A LDA/VEM topic model with 20 topics"

saveRDS(bills_lda, "data/bills_lda_model.rds")
```

#### After Fitting k=20, Validate Substantively

The perplexity plot justifies the range — the final decision must come from reading the top terms:

```{r}
# Top 10 terms per topic — inspect for substantive coherence
tidy(bills_lda, matrix = "beta") %>%
  group_by(topic) %>%
  slice_max(beta, n = 10) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(term = reorder_within(term, beta, topic)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(beta, term, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~topic, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_y_reordered() +
  labs(title = "Top 10 Terms per Topic", x = "Beta", y = NULL)
```

This is a strong and largely interpretable set of topics — k=20 is confirmed. However, there are 6 artifact topics that need to be addressed before saving. Here is a full diagnostic breakdown:

#### Topic Labels — Substantively Meaningful (14 topics)

| Topic | Key Terms                                   | Suggested Label                      |
| ----- | ------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| 1     | involuntary, inpatient, rcw, hospital       | Involuntary/Inpatient Treatment (WA) |
| 2     | felony, possess, dealer, dangerous          | Drug Crime & Penalties               |
| 3     | pregnant, prenatal, screening, nurse        | Maternal & Prenatal Health           |
| 8     | laboratory, dentist, institutions, devices  | Healthcare Facilities & Licensing    |
| 9     | governor, appointed, duties, agencies       | Advisory Bodies & Task Forces        |
| 10    | professional, degree, examination, clinical | Workforce Licensing                  |
| 11    | equity, procedure, cost, addressing         | Policy Equity & Conditions           |
| 12    | audit, comptroller, fund, paid              | Budget & Fiscal Provisions           |
| 13    | licensure, disclosure, records, immunity    | Licensing & Privacy Records          |
| 15    | corporation, controlled, body, devices      | Corporate & Regulatory               |
| 16    | coverage, insurance, medicaid, benefits     | Insurance & Medicaid Coverage        |
| 18    | education, school, students, community      | Education & Community                |
| 19    | court, criminal, attorney, convicted        | Criminal Justice                     |
| 4     | business, control, receipt, entity          | General Regulatory Procedures        |

Topics 16, 18, and 19 are your strongest SDOH-relevant topics for RQ4.

#### Artifact Topics — Require Action (6 topics)

| Topic | Evidence                                   | Cause                                              |
| ----- | ------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------- |
| 5     | fiscal, digest, counsel's, text            | Bill digest/fiscal note headers — CA/IL formatting |
| 6     | f.s., additions, deletions, amending       | Florida Statutes citation format                   |
| 7     | commonwealth, printer's, referred          | Pennsylvania boilerplate                           |
| 14    | ilcs, p.a., synopsis, source               | Illinois Compiled Statutes formatting              |
| 17    | assem, bly, italics, brackets, underscored | Pure markup artifact — worst offender              |
| 20    | lrb, bureau, referred                      | Wisconsin Legislative Reference Bureau             |

Topic 17 is the most serious — assem + bly are split tokens from the word "assembly" being broken across a line, meaning your OCR/text processing split some words. The others are state-specific legislative header terms that passed through your custom stopword list.

Accept k=20, label artifact topics as "procedural_artifact", and exclude them from all RQ4 comparisons.

#### Step 1: Create the Topic Labels Table

```{r}
topic_labels <- tibble(
  topic = 1:20,
  topic_label = c(
    "Involuntary & Inpatient Treatment",   # 1
    "Drug Crime & Penalties",              # 2
    "Maternal & Prenatal Health",          # 3
    "General Regulatory Procedures",       # 4
    "procedural_artifact",                 # 5 — bill digest headers
    "procedural_artifact",                 # 6 — Florida Statutes format
    "procedural_artifact",                 # 7 — Pennsylvania boilerplate
    "Healthcare Facilities & Licensing",   # 8
    "Advisory Bodies & Task Forces",       # 9
    "Workforce Licensing",                 # 10
    "Policy Equity & Conditions",          # 11
    "Budget & Fiscal Provisions",          # 12
    "Licensing & Privacy Records",         # 13
    "procedural_artifact",                 # 14 — Illinois ILCS format
    "Corporate & Regulatory",              # 15
    "Insurance & Medicaid Coverage",       # 16
    "procedural_artifact",                 # 17 — markup artifacts
    "Education & Community",               # 18
    "Criminal Justice",                    # 19
    "procedural_artifact"                  # 20 — Wisconsin LRB format
  ),
  is_artifact = c(
    FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE,
    TRUE, TRUE, TRUE,
    FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE,
    TRUE,
    FALSE,
    FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE
  )
)
```

#### Step 2: Join Labels to Topic Table

```{r}
topic_table <- tidy(bills_lda, matrix = "gamma") %>%
  rename(
    bill_id = document,
    topic   = topic,
    gamma   = gamma
  ) %>%
  mutate(bill_id = as.numeric(bill_id)) %>%
  left_join(topic_labels, by = "topic")
```

#### Step 3: Validate

```{r}
# Confirm label join worked
count(topic_table, topic, topic_label, is_artifact)

# Confirm artifact vs. substantive split
topic_table %>%
  distinct(topic, topic_label, is_artifact) %>%
  count(is_artifact)
# Should show: FALSE = 14, TRUE = 6

# Quick gamma check — dominant topic per bill (substantive only)
topic_table %>%
  filter(!is_artifact) %>%
  group_by(bill_id) %>%
  slice_max(gamma, n = 1) %>%
  count(topic_label, sort = TRUE)
```

#### Step 4: Save

```{r}
write_csv(topic_table, "data/topic_table.csv")
saveRDS(topic_table, "data/topic_table.rds")
saveRDS(bills_lda, "data/bills_lda_model.rds")
write_csv(topic_labels, "data/topic_labels.csv")
```
